# Josh Morden — Personal Landing Page

*Replace "Josh Morden" with your own name if reusing this template.*

## Description

*2–4 sentences describing what the project does, why it exists, and who it is for.*

A professional single-page landing page built for Josh Morden, a Business Analytics and Information Systems student at the University of Iowa. The site serves as a live demonstration of digital product management skills — showcasing academic projects, technical proficiencies, and contact information in one responsive, accessible page. It was created as a capstone deliverable for BAIS:3300 Digital Product Management to prove that a resume alone cannot demonstrate the ability to build and ship a digital product. The target audience is recruiters, hiring managers, and peer data professionals seeking candidates who can bridge data analysis with product delivery.

## Features

*Update this list to match your own feature set.*

- Sticky navigation bar with smooth-scroll anchor links to every section
- Hero section with circular professional headshot and tagline
- Skills section with color-coded pill badges separating technical tools from methodologies
- Three project cards in a responsive two-column grid, each with a tool chip row
- Contact section with labeled icon badges linking to LinkedIn, GitHub, and university email
- Fully responsive layout from 320 px wide with no horizontal scrolling
- WCAG 2.2 Level AA accessible — semantic HTML5, logical heading hierarchy, descriptive alt text, and keyboard navigability
- Zero dependencies — no frameworks, no JavaScript, no build step required

## Tech Stack

*Add or remove rows to match your own stack.*

| Technology | Purpose |
|---|---|
| HTML5 | Page structure and semantic markup |
| CSS3 | All visual styling via external stylesheet |
| Google Fonts (Inter) | Typography — headings and body copy |
| shields.io | README status badges |
| Python `http.server` | Local development server (Replit environment) |

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

*List everything a user needs before they can run the project locally.*

- A modern web browser (Chrome, Safari, or Firefox)
- [Git](https://git-scm.com/downloads) installed on your machine
- No additional runtimes or package managers required — this is a static site

### Installation

*Numbered steps to clone and run the project locally.*

1. Clone the repository:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/mordenjj/personal_landing_page.git
   ```
2. Navigate into the project folder:
   ```bash
   cd personal_landing_page
   ```
3. Open `index.html` directly in your browser, **or** serve it locally with Python:
   ```bash
   python3 -m http.server 8080
   ```
4. Visit `http://localhost:8080` in your browser.

## Usage

*Explain how to use the application once it is running.*

Open the page in any browser to view the landing page. Use the sticky navigation bar at the top to jump between the About, Skills, Projects, and Contact sections. All external links (LinkedIn, GitHub, email) open in a new tab. To personalize the page for your own use, edit the text content in `index.html` and swap out `images/headshot.jpeg` with your own photo — no build tools needed.

## Project Structure

```
personal_landing_page/        # Repository root
├── index.html                # Single-page HTML5 application (all content)
├── css/
│   └── stylesheet.css        # All styles — no inline CSS anywhere in the project
├── images/
│   └── headshot.jpeg         # Professional headshot referenced in the hero section
├── PRD.md                    # Product Requirement Document defining goals and scope
├── STANDARDS.md              # Technical and design standards for every coding session
├── README.md                 # This file
└── .github/
    └── workflows/            # Azure Static Web Apps CI/CD pipeline (not used on Replit)
```

## Changelog

*Update the version number and date for each release.*

### v1.0.0 — March 11, 2026

- Initial release of the personal landing page
- Sticky navigation bar with anchor links
- Hero section with circular headshot, name, and professional tagline
- About section with bio drawn from resume and PRD
- Skills section with technical and methodology pill badges
- Three project cards (NCAA Basketball Viewership, Olympic Swimming Database, NYPD Arrest Analysis)
- Contact section with LinkedIn, GitHub, and university email badges
- Earthy red, tan, and gold color palette
- Fully responsive from 320 px; WCAG 2.2 Level AA compliant

## Known Issues / To-Do

*Check off items as they are completed, or add new ones as they are discovered.*

- [ ] Add a favicon so the browser tab displays a custom icon instead of the default
- [ ] The Projects section currently links to no external repositories — add GitHub links when project repos are made public
- [ ] No dark mode support — a `prefers-color-scheme: dark` media query could improve accessibility for low-light users
- [ ] `STANDARDS.md` Section 1 (Project Overview) and color/font rows are still template placeholders and should be filled in with finalized values

## Roadmap

*Describe features or improvements planned for hypothetical future versions.*

- Add a "Work Experience" section to reflect professional roles (bookkeeping, line cook) alongside academic projects
- Integrate a GitHub API widget to display live repository statistics (stars, forks, last commit) on each project card
- Add a subtle scroll-triggered animation library (e.g., AOS) to improve the visual experience on first load
- Implement a contact form backed by a serverless function (e.g., Formspree or Azure Functions) to replace the email link
- Publish the site to a custom domain with HTTPS via Azure Static Web Apps

## Contributing

*Explain how others can contribute to the project.*

This is a personal academic project, but suggestions and improvements are welcome. To contribute:

1. Fork the repository on GitHub.
2. Create a new branch for your change: `git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name`
3. Make your edits, following the standards defined in `STANDARDS.md`.
4. Commit your changes with a descriptive message: `git commit -m "Add: description of change"`
5. Push the branch to your fork: `git push origin feature/your-feature-name`
6. Open a Pull Request against the `main` branch and describe what you changed and why.
